content.full_text:
"1 / 300
92011

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration
(916) 322-5660

Executive/Legal
322-5901

Conflict of Interest
322-6441

322-6444

November 8, 1977

Eric Lauterer
City Attorney
City of Garden Grove
11391 Acacia Parkway
Garden Grove, CA 92640

A-77-541

Re: Opinion Request No. 77-026

Dear Mr. Lauterer:

This is in response to your letter to the Commission
dated September 6, 1977, requesting an opinion concerning the
development of a Conflict of Interest Code for the Inter-County
Airport Authority. The Commission has determined not to issue
a formal opinion in this matter because it does not present a
substantial question of interpretation under the Political Re-
form Act. Also, as will appear from the advice rendered here-
in, it appears that some of the questions raised by your letter
are premature at this time. However, I trust the following in-
formal advice will enable you to resolve your questions.

Your first question is whether the Authority is a public
body of the nature and type required to adopt a Conflict of In-
terest Code. After reviewing the Joint Powers Agreement between
the various cities which establishes the Authority and the ap-
plicable sections of the Government Code the answer must be in
the affirmative. We think this conclusion is dictated by our de-
cision in the opinion requested by Samuel Siegel, 3 FPPC Opinions
62, a copy of which is enclosed.

In answer to your fourth question, the appropriate
governmental agency to approve the code is this Commission.
Government Code Section 820111/ provides in relevant part that:

"Code reviewing body" means:

(a) The Commission, with respect to the Conflict
of Interest Code of a state agency, a county board

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

-Eric Lauterer
P. 2
November 8, 1977

of supervisors, a city council or any local gov-
ernment agency with jurisdiction in more than one
county;....

We believe the Inter-County Airport Authority is a "local gov-
ernment agency with jurisdiction in more than one county" with-
in the meaning of the cited subsection. While it may be that the
Airport Authority (and many other similar joint powers agencies)
may lease or own property in only a single county, the cities
or other governmental bodies that come together to form-the
Authority are located in different counties. We think this
causes the jurisdiction of the Authority to extend across county
lines.

It is the responsibility of this Commission, therefore,
to establish a deadline for submission of the code and to approve
the code within the framework of Sections 87303 and 87309 of the
Act. The Commission's staff is currently formulating a list of
joint power agencies required to submit Conflict of Interest Codes,
and it is anticipated that notice of dates for submission of codes
will be sent to the appropriate agencies in the next few months.

In light of this analysis your second and third ques-
tions would appear to be somewhat premature pending development
of the Authority's code and a hearing on that code before the Com-
mission. However, I would offer the following general observations.
With respect to your second question, in approving previously sub-
mitted codes of state agencies, the Commission has taken the po-
sition that alternate members of boards and commissions should be
included as designated employees (assuming they meet all other
criteria for designation) if they have permanent status. They
need not be included if they merely sit as a member's designee on
an ad hoc basis.

Your third question concerning what disclosure should be
required of Authority employees must be resolved as an initial
matter by the Authority itself when it develops its code and
then by the Commission when it reviews the code. See Section
87309. In formulating the appropriate disclosure requirements
you should be guided by the provisions of Section 87302 of the
Act.

In addition, the Commission has sought to enunciate
general interpretations of the concepts of "foreseeably" and
"materially" in policy statements which I have enclosed for your
review.

Your final question asks where the statements of economic
interest which will eventually be submitted under the Authority's

Eric Lauterer
P. 3
November 8, 1977


code should be filed. Under Section 81005(g), the following pro-
cedure should be followed. Members of the Authority (or alter-
nates where appropriate) who are designated employees under the
code should file with the Authority which shall "make and retain
a copy and forward the original to the code reviewing body." In
this case, the Commission. Section 81005(g). Other desig-
nated employees under the code will merely file their statements
with a person who is designated as the filing officer in the
Authority's code. See Sections 82027 and 81010.

     I trust this answers your questions for the time being.
Thank you for writing the Commission. If we can be of further
assistance in this matter please feel free to call on Kenneth
Goshorn of the Conflict of Interest Division at (916) 322-6444.
If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you
may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:KG:jo

Enclosures

sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney requests clarification on establishing a Conflict of Interest Code for the Inter-County Airport Authority, including whether the Authority must adopt such a code, which agency should approve it, whether alternate board members should be designated employees, what disclosure requirements apply, and where filed statements should be submitted.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined not to issue a formal opinion but provided informal advice: the Inter-County Airport Authority is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code and must submit it to the FPPC for approval as it is a local government agency with multi-county jurisdiction. Alternate members with permanent status should be included as designated employees, disclosure requirements must be determined by the Authority per Section 87302, and filed statements follow specific procedures depending on whether filers are Authority members or other designated employees.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an informal advice letter rather than formal opinion (explicitly stated). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers present; questions and conclusions are embedded in narrative text. Opinion Request No. 77-026 dated September 6, 1977. Multiple references to Government Code sections and prior FPPC opinions included.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The City Attorney requests clarification on establishing a Conflict of Interest Code for the Inter-County Airport Authority, including whether the Authority must adopt such a code, which agency should approve it, whether alternate board members should be designated employees, what disclosure requirements apply, and where filed statements should be submitted.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC determined not to issue a formal opinion but provided informal advice: the Inter-County Airport Authority is required to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code and must submit it to the FPPC for approval as it is a local government agency with multi-county jurisdiction. Alternate members with permanent status should be included as designated employees, disclosure requirements must be determined by the Authority per Section 87302, and filed statements follow specific procedures depending on whether filers are Authority members or other designated employees.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the City Attorney of Garden Grove's inquiry regarding establishment of a Conflict of Interest Code for the Inter-County Airport Authority by declining to issue a formal opinion but providing informal advice on five substantive questions concerning the Authority's status, code approval authority, employee designation, disclosure requirements, and filing procedures.
